text: ALCIRA, a town of Spain, in Valencia, at the confluence of the Magro and the Xucar, 18 m. N by E of San Felipe. Pop. 8,400. Silk and rice are extensively cultivated in the environs. It was the Sucro of the Carthaginians, the Sætabicula of the Romans, and Algezira of the Moors.
ALCISTON, a p. in Sussex, 43 m. NE of Seaford. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 275.—Also a hundred of the same co. embracing the three parishes of Alciston, Alfriston, and Lullington.
ALCMAER. See ALKMAAR.
ALCOBA (SIERRA DE), a chain of mountains of Portugal, in Beira, running N and S between the Mondego and the Vouga rivers.

ALCOBAÇA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, near the confluence of the Alcoa and Baça rivers; 18 m. N of Obidos, and 5 m. from the sea. Pop. 1,800. There is a magnificent Bernardine monastery here, founded in the 12th cent., and forming the general place of sepulture for the kings of Portugal. Its western front, including the church, which is in the centre, extends 620 ft. The kitchen is nearly 100 ft. long, and 63 ft. from the floor to the intrados of the vaulted ceiling.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the r. bank of the Tocantines, 160 m. SSE of Para.—There is another town of the same name in the Brazilian prov. of Seguro, 70 m. N of San Joze do Porto Alegre, near the mouth of a river of the same name, on its northern bank. Pop. 900. It carries on some trade in mandioc flour.
ALCOBENDAS, or ALCOVENDAS, a town of Spain, 9 m. NNE of Madrid. Pop. 2,000.
ALCOBER, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, on the Angura, 10 m. NNW of Tarragona. Pop. 3,000.
ALCOCER, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 80 m. W of Alcira, on the l. bank of the Xucar.—Also a town in Estremadura, 50 m. E of Merida.
ALCOENTRE, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 20 m. W by S of Santarem.

ALCOLEA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Seville, in Andalusia, 6 m. N of Carmona, near the r. bank of the Guadalquivir, which is here crossed by a fine bridge.—Also a town in Aragon, on the Cinca river, 15 m. S by W of Barbastro.—Also a town in Old Castile, in the prov. of Soria, 12 m. W of Maranchon. A few miles to the W of this place culture ceases, and the country is abandoned to the ilex and quercus.—Townsend.
ALCOLEA-DE-TAJO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Toledo, on the r. bank of the Tagus, 20 m. SW of Talavera.
ALCONBURY, a p. in Huntingdonshire, 4 m. NW of Huntingdon. Area 3,700 acres. Pop. 823.
ALCONBURY WESTON, a p. in Huntingdonshire, 6 m. NW of Huntingdon. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 491.
ALCONCHEL, a fortified v. of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Badajoz, on the Taliga river, 12 m. S of Olivença.

ALCORA, a town of Spain, in Valencia, 10 m. NNW of Castellon de la Plana, on the Rio Lucena. Pop. 2,500. It is celebrated for the manufacture of earthenware. ALCORÇON, "formerly a town, at present an insignificant v. with 250 inhabitants" [Laborde], in the prov. of Madrid, in a waste uncultivated tract of level ground 6 m. to the W of the Spanish capital.
ALCOUCHETTE, a fortified v. of Portugal, in Estremadura, on the l. bank of the Tagus, 10 m. ENE of Lisbon.

ALCOURA, a Maronite v. in the Libanus district of Syria, consisting of about 100 houses, and tributary to the governor of Djebail. It is situated at the foot of a lofty precipice; and its elevation above sealevel must be very considerable, as Colonel Squire found no leaves on the mulberry trees, and silk-worms still in the egg here, on the 24th of April, while at Tripoli they were already brought into the gardens.
ALCOUTIM, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, on a small island formed by the Guadiana, 16 m. from its mouth, 24 m. N of Tavira. Pop. 1,453. It lies opposite the Spanish v. of San Lucar.

ALCOY, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. of San Felipe, 21 m. N of Alicant, near the source of the river of the same name. Pop. 18,000. [Moller], 15,000 [Rudtorffer]. It is a place of considerable trade, and has numerous woollen, soap, and paper manufactories. The peladillas de Alcoy, a kind of sugared almond-plums, are celebrated. This place, unlike almost any other town in Spain, is said to be rapidly increasing in population and in buildings. The surrounding country is fertile, and well-clothed with cypresses, figs, and almond-trees. Iron occurs in the vicinity.

ALCUBIERRE (SIERRA DE), a secondary chain of mountains in Aragon, running from NW to SE between the vallies of the Ebro and the Isuela, and separating the provinces of Huesca and Saragossa. A town of the same name lies on its northern declivity, 20 m. NE of Saragossa.

ALCUDIA, a river of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of La Mancha, rising to the NE of Sacernela, and flowing in a SW course to the Guadalmez, which it joins on the r. bank 10 m. S by W of Almadan.—Also a town of Majorca, on a neck of land between a bay of the same name and the bay of Pollenza, on the NE coast of the island, 32 m. NE of Palma. It stands on an eminence about a mile from the sea, and is surrounded with high walls. Some centuries ago it was a flourishing place, but it is now in a state of utter decay, and its pop. does not exceed 1,000. The bay of A., though open to the NE and E, is preferable to that of Pollenza, and has from 3 to 45 fathoms water at a cable's length from the shore.
ALCUDIA-DE-CARLET, a town of Spain, in Valencia, 8 m. NW of San Felipe. Pop. 2,000. It was the birth-place of the painter Joseph Vergara, and of his brother Ignacio Vergara, the sculptor.
ALCUEZAR, or ALQUEZAR, a town of Spain, in Aragon, 12 m. NNW of Barbastro.
ALD, a river of Suffolk, which, rising near Framlingham, runs SE, and falls into the sea, by a long winding estuary, between Aldborough and Orford.

ALDABRA, or Arcos, two islets of the Indian ocean, in the N entrance of the Mozambique channel, in S lat. 9° 26', E long. 46° 35' [Raper], S lat. 9° 22', E long. 46° 50' [Parl. Paper, 1827]. They are uninhabited and uninhabitable, consisting merely of a mass of great blocks of coral intersected by canals. Their circumference is about 24 m.

ALDA'N, a river of Siberia, a confluent of the Lena. It rises on the northern declivity of the Yablonoi Khrebet, between 127° and 129° E long, and runs in a NNE course nearly 400 m. to Aldanskaja. ALD It then turns NW by N, and after flowing more than 100 m. in that direction, bends WSW, and pursues that direction to its junction with the Lena, on the r. bank, 60 m. below Yakutsk. Wrangel found it 11 verst in breadth, with a rapid current at 90 versts above its junction with the Lena. Aldanskaja Perewos, in the valley of the A., is 424 ft. above sea-level. [Erman.]—A mountain-range, deriving its name from this river, rises with a steep ascent, from its eastern banks. Mount Kapitan, in this range, has an alt. of 4,263 ft. above sea-level; and from this mountain the surface of the country as far E as Ketanda, about 100 m. from Okotsk, has an average elevation of 2,500 ft. [Erman.] Cochrane says that the whole distance from Okotsk to Yakutsk-with the exception of a portage across a chain of mountains which confine the Arko, a large stream uniting with the Okota-might be accomplished in canoes by means of the Udoma a tributary of the Aldan. When the ice breaks upwhich usually happens in the month of May-vast masses are borne down the course of the A. with tremendous velocity.

ALDBOROUGH, or ALDEBURGH, a p. and seaport in Suffolk, 22 m. ENE of Ipswich. Area of p. 1,130 acres. Pop. 1,157. The town, until disfranchised by the Reform act, returned two members to parliament. About 46 vessels averaging 52 tons burden belong to this port, which is now subject to the port of Woodbridge in all revenue-matters. A. was the birth-place of the poet Crabbe, on 24 Dec., 1754. -Also a p. and market-town in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area of p. 8,750 acres. Pop. 2,424. The town is 16 m. NW by W of York, on the S bank of the Ure. It also returned two representatives until disfranchised by the Reform act.—Also a p. in Norfolk, 4 m. N of Aylsham. Area 590 acres. Pop. 293.
ALDBOURNE, a p. and v. in Wiltshire. Area of p. 8,060 acres. Pop. 1,556. The v. is 6 m. NE of Marlborough, on a small tributary of the Kennett.
ALDBROUGH, a p. and v. in the E. R. of Yorkshire. Area of p. 5,240 acres. Pop. 1,190. The v. is 11 m. NE by E from Kingston-upon-Hull. Pop. of v. and township in 1841, 845.
ALDBURY, a p. in Hertford, 2 m. NE of Tring. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 790.—See also Albury.
ALDEA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 14 m. ENE of Sabugal, on the Spanish frontier.
ALDEA-DEL-CANO, a town of Spain, in Estre madura, 18 m. S of Caceres, on the Ayuela river.
ALDEA-GALLEGA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, on a creek of the estuary of the Tagus, 11 m. N by W of Setubal. Pop. 4,000.
ALDEA-GALLEGA-DA-MERCIANA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 12 m. WNW of Alenquer.
ALDEA-DOS-MANDRUCOS, a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the l. bank of the Tapajos, 75 m. above Aveiro.
ALDEA-EL-MURO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Soria, in Old Castile, 8 m. E by N of Soria. It is supposed by some to be the Augustobriga of Ptolemy.
ALDEA-NHAMBARANAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the E side of a lake formed by the Mauheguaçu, 60 m. SSW of Villa da Rainha.
ALDEA-NUEVA-DEL-CAMINO, a town of Spain, on the Estremadura, 15 m. NNW of Plasencia, on the Ambroz. Pop. 1,500.
ALDEA-DEL-RIO, a v. of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Cordova, on the Guadalquivir, 12 m. SW of Andujar.
ALDEA-VIEYA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Avila, in Old Castile, 15 m. NE of Avila. Pop. 1,200.

ALDEBY, or ALDEBURGH, a p. of Norfolk, 23 m. NE of Beccles. Area 2,690 acres. Pop. 496. ALDEKERCK a v. of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, 34 m. N of Aix-la-Chapelle. Pop. | 600, chiefly linen and silk weavers.
ALDEN, a town of New York, U. S., in Erie co., 22 m. E of Buffalo. Pop. 1,984.—Also an island on the W coast of Norway, in N lat. 61° 19'.
ALDENHAM, a p. and v. in Hertfordshire. Area 5,830 acres. Pop. 1,662. The v. is 2½ m. from Watford station on the London and Birmingham railroad.

ALDENHOVEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, 5 m. WSW of Juliers. Pop. 1,100. On the 1st of March 1793, and again on the 2d of October 1794, the Austrian and French forces encountered each other in the vicinity of this town.

ALDERBURY, a p. and town in Wiltshire. Area of p. 3,950 acres. Pop. 1,440. The town is 3 m. SE by E of Salisbury. Pop. 700.—The hundred of A. lies on the E side of the Avon. Area 31,760 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,469; in 1841, 4,979.
ALDERFORD, a p. in Norfolk, 3 m. E by S of Reepham. Area 320 acres. Pop. 44.
ALDERHOLM, an island of Sweden, at the mouth of the river Geffle, 80 m. N of Stockholm.

ALDERLEY, a p. and v. of Gloucestershire, 2 m. SSE of Wotton-under-Edge. Area of p. 730 acres. Pop. 174. Sir Matthew Hale was born at the v. of A. in 1609.—Also a p. in Cheshire. Area 6,240 acres. Pop. 1,538. The Manchester and Birmingham railway intersects this parish.
ALDERMAN JONES'S SOUND, a bay on the NW side of Baffin's bay, in N lat. 76° 40', W long. 79° 30'. Its entrance is between Cape Hardwick on the N, and Cape Lindsay on the S.
ALDERMASTON, a p. and t. in Berkshire, 8 m. E of Newbury. Area of p. 2,240 acres. Pop. 662.

ALDERMEN ISLANDS, or COURT OF ALDERMEN, a group of islands off the E coast of the north island of New Zealand, in S lat. 36° 57'. "They lie in the compass of about half-a-league every way, and 5 leagues from the main, between which and them lie other islands, most of them barren rocks, of which there is great variety. Some of them are as small in compass as the Monument of London, but rise to a much greater height; and some of them are inhabited."—Cook.
ALDERMINSTER, a p. of Worcestershire, but locally in Warwickshire, 5 m. SSE of Stratford-upon- Avon. Area 3,480 acres. Pop. 508.

ALDERNEY, or AURIGNI, a small island belonging to Great Britain, off the coast of Normandy, to which duchy it once belonged. It is 3½ m. long, and 8 m. in circumference; 8½ m. W by N from Cape La Hogue; 18 m. NE by N from Guernsey, and 60 m. from Portland-bill, the nearest point of the English coast. With Guernsey, Jersey, and Sark, it forms the only part of the French possessions of William the Conqueror now remaining under the government of England. It is a civil dependency of Guernsey; and, with Jersey and Guernsey, is ecclesiastically annexed to the see of Winchester. The town, as it is called, is situated in the centre of the island, and bears its name; but is sometimes known as St. Anne. The air is salubrious; the soil fertile and cultivated; and the island exports grain to England, and is celebrated for its breed of milk-cows. Lobsters are abundant, and form an article of export to England. The French language and manners predominate in this island. Four small vessels belong to A., two of which ply regularly to Guernsey. The chief port, that of Braye, is a tidal harbour, m. to the NE of the town. Its pier is 408 ft. in length, and 46 ft. high. Pop. in 1801, 1,154; in 1831, 1,045; in 1841, 1,030. The decrease is owing to emigration. Inglis says that the pop. may be generally characterized as poor and improvident, Houses in 1831, 217; in 1841, 225.—The strait called the Race of Alderney, which separates this island from Cape La Hogue on the French coast, is very dangerous in stormy weather on account of a range of rocks called the Caskets. On these rocks Prince William, son of Henry I., and his suite, perished in 1119; and in 1744 the Victory of 110 guns foundered on this dangerous ledge, when the entire crew of 1,100 men, with Admiral Sir John Balchen, perished.—Three light-houses, with revolving lights, are built on the highest of these rocks, at 120 it above the mean level of the sea, in N lat. 49 deg. 42 min. 17 sec., W long. 2 deg. 23 min. 34 sec. They are placed in a triangular form; and exhibit alternately, every 15 seconds, a bright ray of light in every direction.
ALDERNEY (New), an island in the Pacific | ALE ocean, in S lat. 11° 10', E long. 165° 19', discovered by Carteret in 1767. It is 10 m. long, and 5 m. broad.
ALDERSHOTT, a p. of Hampshire, 3 m. NE by N of Farnham. Area 4,070 acres. Pop. 685.

ALDERTON, a p. of Gloucestershire, 7 m. W of Tewkesbury. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 411.—Also a p. of Northamptonshire, 3 m. SE of Towcester. Area 910 acres. Pop. 166.—Also a p. of Suffolk, 7 m. SE by S of Woodbridge. Area 2,680 acres. Pop. 620. -Also a p. of Wiltshire. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 183.
ALDFORD, a p. of Cheshire, on the Dee, 6 m. S by E of Chester. Area 2,820 acres. Pop. 835.
ALDHAM, a p. of Essex, 43 m. ENE of Great Coggeshall. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 382.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 2 m. NE of Hadleigh. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 293.
ALDINGBOURN, a p. of Sussex, 4 m. E of Chichester. Area 3,080 acres. Pop. 772.
ALDINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, on the Baar river, SE of Rottweil. Pop. 1,313.—Also a town on the Ammer, NW of Tubingen.—Also a town on the Neckar, SE of Ludwigsburg. Pop. 1,194.
ALDINGHAM, a p. of Lancashire, on Morecombe bay, 6 m. S of Ulverstone. Area 4,680 acres. Pop. 907. The sea has made great inroads on this p. within the last two or three centuries.
ALDINGTON, a p. of Kent, 6 m. WNW of Hythe. Area 3,420 acres. Pop. 733.
ALDIS PEAK, a sharp-pointed mountain in the Expedition range in Australia, in about S lat. 24° 523'.—Leichardt.
ALDRIDGE, a p. of Staffordshire, 3 m. ENE of Walsall. Area 7,930 acres. Pop. 2,094.
ALDRINGHAM, a p. on the E coast of Suffolk, 2½ m. N by W of Aldborough. Area 610 acres. Pop. 401.

ALDSTONE, or Alston Moor, a p. and t. of Cumberland, consisting of a narrow valley surrounded by fells or high lands, and having itself an elevation of 1,000 ft. above sea-level. Area of p. 35,060 acres. Pop. in 1821, 5,699; in 1841, 6,062. It is a rich mineral district. In 1768 the average produce of the lead-mines in this p. was 167,544 cwt.; in 1814, the yield was 91,968 cwt. These mines were formerly the property of the Derwentwater family; but on the forfeiture of the last earl of that name were granted to Greenwich hospital. The intellectual condition of the miners and population generally of this p. is said to be decidedly superior to that of the labouring pop. in any other district of England.—The town of A. is situated near the confluence of the Nent and the South Tyne, 25 m. ESE of Carlisle.
ALDSWORTH, a p. of Gloucestershire, 4½ m. SE of Northleach. Area 3,460 acres. Pop. 365.

ALDUDES, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Basses Pyrénées, on the Ereux, 12 m. WSW of St.-Jean-Pied-de-Port. Pop. 2,822. Elevation above sea-level 1,243 ft. On the 3d of June, 1794, the French army forced the defile leading from this v. into Navarre.

ALDWINKLE, the name of two p. in Northamptonshire, of which the one distinguished by the appellation ALL SAINTS had a pop. in 1841 of 183; and the other, St. Peter's, of 183. The area of the united parishes is 2,450 acres. They are respectively 4 m. and 3 m. NE of Thrapston.
ALDWORTH, or ALLDER, a p. of Berkshire, 4 m. E by S of East Ilsley. Area 1,960 acres. Pop. 315.

ALE, a small stream of Berwickshire, which rises on the northern skirts of Coldinghame p., and, running SE, joins the Eye 1 m. above Eyemouth.—Also a stream of Selkirkshire, flowing from Alemoor loch NE to the Teviot.
ALED, a small stream of N. Wales, in Denbighshire, flowing from Llyn Aled in a N direction to the Elwy.
ALEDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 19 m. SW of Murcia. Pop. 2,400.

ALEGRE, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, which rises in the Agoapehy mountains, and flows NW to the Guapore, which it joins on the r. bank, 25 m. above Villa Bella. It is connected near its head by a portage of 3 m. with the Agoapehy river flowing SE to the Paraguay.—Also a town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, arrond. of Puy. Pop. 900.

ALEGRE (MORRO), a hill on the N coast of Brazil, in S lat. 2° 20½, W long. 43° 13'.—There is another hill known as Mount Alegre, in S lat. 2° 17½', W long. 44° 20'. ALEGRE (SAN - JOZE - DO - PORTO) - contracted

PORTALEGRE, a sea-port of Brazil, in the prov. of Seguro, at the mouth of the Mueury. Pop. 6,111. It exports farinha, flax, and timber. From this town a road runs to Villa do Principe in Minas Geraes.
ALEGRETE, a fortified town of Portugal, in Alemtejo, on the Caïa, 9 m. SE of Portalegre. Pop. 1,500.
ALEGRIA, a town of Spain in Guipuscoa, in the prov. and 18 m. SSW of San Sebastian, on the l. bank of the Oria, at the foot of Mount Alduba.
ALEI, a river of Siberia, rising in the district of Büsk, in the gov. of Tomsk, and flowing, in a course of 200 m. from SE to NW, into the Ovi, 40 m. above Barnaul.
ALEIN, a town of France, in the arrond. of Arles, dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, 6 m. E of Eyguyères. Pop. 1,050.

ALEIS, a town of Upper Nubia, on the r. bank of the Bahr-el-Abiad or White Nile, in N lat. 13° 23'. The river immediately above A. is 3 m. wide, and contains a number of low islands. Opposite the town it is narrower. Linant says the town appeared to him of considerable size, and built like Sennaar, but as being chiefly in ruins, having been long since abandoned, except by a few families, whom the caravantrade between Sennaar and El Obeid, the capital of Kordofan, attracts to this place. The Shilluk territory begins to the S of A.

ALEIXO, or ALEXO (SAN), two islets off the coast of Pernambuco in Brazil, 16 m. SW of Cape St. Augustin; in S lat. 8° 36', W long. 35° 0'. There is no passage between these islands and the adjacent continent.

ALEKIA'M, or Alckiam, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in the district of Karahissar, 68 m. ESE of Kutaia, near the site of the ancient Orcistus, the ruins of which may be traced on a rising ground a few miles to the S of a head-branch of the Sangarius.
ALEKSANDRIA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Kherson, on the Inguletz. Pop. 2,500.
ALEKSANDROV, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Caucasus, 60 m. SE of Stavropol.—Also a town in the gov. of Vladimir, on the Seraïa. Pop. 3,000.

ALEKSANDROVKA, a v. of Russia, in the gov. and 25 m. E by S of Iekaterinoslav, on an affluent of the Dnieper.—Also a v. on the r. bank of the Molotchnouia, in the gov. of Taurida, 80 m. S of Aleksandrovsk.
ALEKSANDROVSK, a town of Russia, on the l. bank of the Dnieper, 50 m. below Iekaterinoslov. Pop. 3,500. It is a place of considerable trade and commerce.
ALEKSANDROW, a town of Poland, in Masovia. Pop. 3,000.
ALEKSIEVSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Simbirsk, 20 m. E by N of Samara.—Also a town near the r. bank of the Volga, in the gov. of Saratov, 35 m. NE by E of Volsk. ALE
ALEKSIEVSKAIA, a town of Russia, in the territory of the Don Cossacks, on a tributary of the Don, 60 m. S by E of Novokhopersk.
ALEKSINE, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 30 m. NW of Tula, on the Occa river.
ALEKSOPOL, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 50 m. S of Poltava, on the Orelly, an affluent of the Dnieper. Pop. 1,200.
ALEKSOTA, a town of Poland, on the l. bank of the Niemen, opposite Kovno, in the gov. of Augostov.
ALEM, a town of Holland, in North Brabant, near the l. bank of the Maas, 7 m. NNE of Bois-le-Duc.
ALEMDAG, or ALEMTAGHE, a mountain of Natolia, lying directly E of Constantinople, and N of Maldepeh, whence much of the timber used at Skutari is obtained.
ALEM KHAN, a v. in Afghanistan, in the Daman, on the r. bank of the Indus.

ALEMOUTH, or ALMMOUTH, a small port on the coast of Northumberland, at the entrance of the Aln, 54 m. ESE of Alnwick, 4 m. NW of Coquet island. Pop. 480. It is in the p. of Lesbury, and near the line of the Newcastle and Berwick railway. A considerable quantity of corn is shipped at this place.
ALEMPIGNON. See NIPIGON.
ALEMQUER, a town of Portuguese Guayana, at the E end of Lake Curuamama, 68 m. WSW of Obidos.—Also a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 23 m. N by E of Lisbon. Pop. 3,200.

ALEMTEJO, or Alentejo, [i. e., Alem Tejo, or 'beyond the Tagus,'] a prov. in the S of Portugal, so named from its relative position to that river. It lies between the parallels of 37° 16' and 39° 30' N; and is bounded on the N by Estremadura and Beira; on the E by the Spanish provinces of Estremadura and Seville; on the S by Algarve; and on the W by the Atlantic ocean and Estremadura. Its length from N to S is 150 m.; its greatest breadth from E to W 85 m.; but it is of very irregular outline. Its superficial area has been estimated at 5,848,320 acres, or above one-fourth of the total superficies of Portugal. The centre is occupied by an elevated sandy plain. The surface generally is hilly. It is intersected throughout its whole length, in a NNE and SSW direction, by a low mountain-range; and its southern boundary is formed by the isolated ridges of Caldeirao and Monchique, the latter of which rises to the height of 4,078 ft., and is the loftiest elevation in the prov. On the N and W, the line of separation from the provinces of Beira and Estremadura is formed, to a considerable extent, by the Tagus; on the NE, the Sever, a tributary of that river, divides it from Spanish Estremadura. Further S, the eastern boundary with Spain is defined by the Gevora and Guadiana. The latter river subsequently enters the prov. in a SE direction. The more central and westerly districts are watered by the Sado, the Serraria, the Odemira, the Caya, the Avis, the Zatas, and the Almanros, all of which rivers generally overflow their banks in winter, and render the adjacent localities extremely marshy. Several lakes occur in the environs of Beja. The lagunes or bays of Pera and Odemira form the only ports which Alemtejo possesses. Mineral springs are numerous. The climate is extremely warm and dry; months often elapse without the refreshment of a shower. The air is pure and salubrious towards the N; but in the central districts, and towards the coast, it is impregnated with malaria. The principal productions are wheat, barley, rice, and fruits. The latter are grown in great abundance and excellence, especially oranges, citrons, figs, pomegranates, and olives. The wines of Senes and Villa de Frades are the most esteemed. In the forests are found the evergreen oak, cork-tree, chestnut, and maritime pine. Julius Cæsar called A. 'the Sicily of Spain,' with allusion to its fertility; but at the present day only two-ninths of the surface are under annual cultivation. Many districts are entirely waste. "The great tenancies that exist, and the peculiar tenures on which property is generally held, are certainly not favourable to an extended cultivation; but unquestionably the arid nature of the soil over large tracts,—their absolute unfitness in many places for the growth of corn,—the scarcity of villages,—the frequent absence of water, -and the general deficiency of hands for agricultural purposes are the principal causes of the neglected state of the rural interest in A." [Lord Carnarvon]. The most fertile districts lie around Evora, Portalegre, Elvas, Villaviciosa, Beja, and the plains of Ourique. The waste lands afford pasturage to large flocks of sheep, the wool of which is of good quality, little inferior to that of the Spanish flocks. Goats and swine are very numerous, but horned cattle are comparatively scarce. The Evora breed of horses is highly esteemed. The rearing of ducks and turkeys forms an important employment in the vicinity of Portalegre. Mines of gold, silver, and other metals, exist; but the scarcity of wood prevents their being wrought. There are quarries of marble of various colours, freestone, slate, and clay. The only manufactures are cloth and pottery. The contraband trade is extremely active on the Spanish frontiers.-A. is divided into 8 comarcas or districts, viz., Evora, Elvas, Portalegre, Ourique, Villaviciosa, Beja, Crato, and Avis. It is bristled with fortresses, of which Elvas and Campo Major are the most important. Evora is the seat of an archbishopric. Three bishops take their titles from Elvas, Portalegre, and Beja. Although the largest, A. is the least populous of the provinces of Portugal, in proportion to its extent. In 1826, the pop. was estimated at 330,000, or little more than one-tenth of that of the kingdom; and in 1838 it was returned at only 314,310; giving 30.64 a square mile, in an estimated superficies of 10,255 sq. m.; while the average density is 97.2. In fact, whole districts are almost without inhabitants, and the pop. is supposed to have diminished during the last century.
ALEN. See AALEN and AHLEN.

ALENCON, a town of France, the cap. of the dep. of the Orne, situated in an extensive plain of the same name, on the NW bank of the Sarthe, near the confluence of that river with the Briente, in N lat. 48° 25' 49", W long. of Paris 2° 14' 52" [Puissant], at an elevation of 136 metres or 446 ft. above sea-level. It is a large, well-built town, with five suburbs, one of which, Montsor, lies on the opposite side of the Sarthe, and is larger than the town itself. Its streets are broad and well-paved, and are adorned with some handsome edifices, of which the principal are, the collegiate church, a Gothic structure of the 16th cent., -the town hall which occupies the site and was constructed from the ruins of the ancient castle of the dukes of Alençon,—the justiciary court, in the vicinity of which is a fine promenade,—the corn-market and two hospitals. It has also a communal college, surmounted by an observatory, a library of 7,000 vols., and two museums. A. is the seat of an assize-court, of the Royal court of Caen, and of the diocese of Siez. It was noted for its manufacture of point lace-Pointe d'Alençon—of which a monopoly of ten years, besides a grant of 36,000 livres, was accorded to the patentee by Colbert; but this manufacture has greatly declined here. Other articles of manufacture are buckram, ticking, linen cloths called Toiles d'Alençon, of which nearly 22,000 pieces are annually made, muslins—the manufacture and embroidery of which afford employment to upwards of 2,000 individuals-jewellery, leather, glass, iron, &c. Its agricultural commerce is limited to cattle, horses, of which it possesses a good breed, goose down and quills, and cider. In the vici- och [M. Guys]. The plague, and a disease, which, from ALE nity are freestone quarries. At Hertre, a v. about 3 m. W of the town, fine quartz crystals-Diamants d'Alençon—are found. The octroi dues of this town amounted in 1833 to 76,590 francs. It has four annual fairs, chiefly for cattle and horses. The pop. was estimated at 11,518 in 1789; and 14,070 in 1846. -A. was formerly the chief town of a province, and subsequently of a duchy, of the same name. It is supposed to have been the ancient capital of the Aulerces. Its early names were Alencium, Alenconium, and Alentio Factorum. In the 9th cent., it consisted of only a few houses, surrounding a strong castle which, in 1026, sustained a siege by Robert, duke of Normandy. In 1417 it fell into the hands of the English; but, after being repeatedly taken and retaken, was ultimately relinquished by them in 1450. In 1525 the duchy was united to the Crown. In 1590 the castle was partially demolished by Henry IV. Alençon has suffered much from religious wars, but escaped in the general massacre of St. Bartholomew by the heroic conduct of its governor Martignon. Hebert, the atrocious revolutionary leader, and editor of the Père Duchèsne journal, was a native of A. Baron Desgenettes, the rationalist, Labillardière and Madame Villedieu, confer upon it a happier celebrity. The arrond. of A. has an area of 103,325 hectares. Its pop. in 1841 was 72,207. It is divided into the 6 cantons of Alençon E and W, Carrouges, Courtomer, Mesle-sur-Sarthe, and Sées.—The canton of A. comprises 25 communes, and had in 1831 a pop. of 27,855. -Dubois, Histoire d'Alençon, 1805.—Patria.—Dictionnaire Geographique.
ALENTO, a river of Naples, in Principato Citra, flowing into the sea between Acciorolo and Pisciotta, after a S course of about 30 m.

ALEOUTES. See ALEUTIAN ARCHIPELAGO. ALEPPO, [Arabic, Haleb,] a pashalik of Asiatic Turkey, forming the northern portion of Syria; bounded on the N by the pash. of Marash; on the E by that of Rakkah; on the S by the pash. of Damascus, and of Tripoli; on the W by the Mediterranean; and on the NW by the pash. of Itchil. It stretches between N lat. 35° 24' and 37°, and E long. 35° 45° and 38° 20'. It is nearly 110 m. in length from N to S, and 120 in breadth. It is intersected, in its northern and eastern districts, by several branches of the Taurus. The pashaliks of Aintab and Killis were joined to that of A. a few years previous to the arrival of Ibrahim Pasha in the country, and the whole formed a superficies of 12,000 sq. m. [Barber]. The Alma-Dagh, or Amanus range, separates it on the NW from the adjacent pash. The mountains of Aintab run towards the NE; and those of Libanus towards Mount Casius on the W. The E and NE districts present extensive tracts of shifting sand. The coast of the Mediterranean forms, in the NW, the gulf of Scanderun; and between Mounts Pierius and Casius the bay of Souvadie; but extensive sandy shallows impede the navigation of the coast and obstruct its ports. The pash. is watered on the E by the Euphrates, which forms the boundary line with the pash. of Rakah; on the SW by the Orontes; and in its central districts by the Koiek, which rises near Aintab, in the N, and falls into a marshy lake to the S of the city of Aleppo. Several lakes occur towards the S and W, of which Geboul, Kinerin, and Antioch are the principal. The climate generally is dry and not insalubrious; excessive heat sometimes prevails in spring and autumn, but in summer it is tempered by the sea-breeze which blows in a NW direction. Heavy rains fall without intermission in spring. Earthquakes are of frequent occurrence. The focus of the great earthquake which devastated this region in 1822 appeared to be between Gisser-il-Shurl, a small v. on the Orontes, and Antiits peculiarity, is called the Mal d'Aleppo—supposed to arise from the insalubrious quality of the waterfrequently commit dreadful ravages. The soil is in general rich and clayey; but the greater part of the land is untilled, and cultivation is chiefly confined to the vicinity of the towns and villages. Agriculture is much neglected in this pash. Wheat is sown about the middle of October, other grains towards the end of January, and barley about a month later. Harvest commences in May, and terminates in the beginning of June. Lint, sesame, saffron, and cotton, are likewise cultivated, but chiefly in the lower districts; and some tobacco is grown on the coast-lands and on Jebel Akra. The gardens-which are generally well-watered-produce oranges, citrons, pomegranates, peaches, apricots, pistachios, almonds, and figs; olives and vines are common near the coast. The wines are not of good quality. Extensive forests cover the mountain-ranges; but their remoteness renders them of little avail to the inhabitants, who substitute brushwood and offal for fuel. Camels and oxen are reared, and employed in tillage and irrigation. Numerous flocks of sheep, remarkable for the large size of their tails, are reared by the nomade inhabitants in the fertile plains by which the mountains are skirted. Vegetation is often greatly devastated by locusts. Quarries of marble, freestone, and lime, exist in the neighbourhood of the city of A.; the same locality produces also fuller's earth, which the inhabitants employ in the manufacture of soap. Salt is obtained from the lake of Geboul. Only one iron mine has been wrought. The exports consist of galls from the interior, cotton, sheep's and goats' wool, drugs, and silk from the valley of Antioch.—The pash. is divided into 5 sanjaks. The principal towns are Aleppo, Antioch, Scanderun or Alexandretta, Beilan, and Aintab. It appears that many once flourishing towns and villages in this pashalik are now in ruins, or in a state of rapid decadence. On the road from Antioch to A. there are only three or four small villages; on the road to Scanderun the pop. is as thinly scattered. The pop., consisting of Turcomans, Arabs, Mutuali, Kurds, and Tchingans, was estimated by Mr. Consul Barker, in 1838, at from 400,000 to 500,000. The average annual revenue of this pash. was raised, under Mahomet Ali's sway, to £139,000 in 1835, and the following year to 15,180,429 plastres, or £152,000, of which 3,673,992 plastres were raised by the ferdeh or capitation tax, upon 69,957 male inhabitants of from 15 years and upwards.— Russell's Natural History of A.—Bowring's Commercial Statistics of Syria.

ALEPPO, or HALEB, the present metropolis of Syria, and, in point of importance, the third city in the Ottoman empire, is situated in the southern part of the pash. of the same name, at the distance of 70 m. in a direct line, or 90 m. by road, from Scanderun, the nearest sea-port; 110 m. from Latakia; and 72 m. from Antioch; in N lat. 36° 11', and E long. 37° 5'. [Beauchamp.] It has an altitude of about 2,000 ft. above sea-level; is built on several hills of unequal height; and is encompassed at the distance of a few miles by a rocky range of hills, pastured by sheep and goats, and destitute of trees. It is surrounded by an ancient wall and ditch, comprising a circuit of 7 m., now to a great extent in a state of dilapidation; but, inclusive of its suburbs, the city forms a circuit of about 7 m. The river Koiek, which has its rise near Aintab, after winding through a plain above the v. of Heilan, 23 hours to the N of A., takes its course through the gardens along the W side of the city; it then flows eastwards, and afterwards turns S and disappears in a tract of marshy ground. Previous to its entering the city, it is reduced—by the distribution of its waters in the irrigation of the numerous gar- * dens which adorn the environs of A .- to a small stream; but in winter it often assumes the appearance of a large river. Like most eastern cities, A. appears to greatest advantage when seen from a distance. The coup d'œil which A., with its mosques, minarets, and cupolas, its lofty castle, and numerous terraces, presents from the adjacent heights, is one of most imposing magnificence; but a striking contrast is felt on entering its walls, from the gloomy aspect of its labyrinths of narrow, dirty streets, and prisonlike houses, presenting high stone walls and dead fronts on each side. In some of its districts, however, the streets are broad and well-paved, and have good foot-paths; and in a few instances they present a fine vista of transverse arches. The houses are built of stone, generally with flat roofs on which the inhabitants sleep in summer. The following recent description of the exterior of the city exhibits a very singular picture:-"I had occasion to mention the low rentals of houses in Aleppo as one of the facilities which the town offers as a place of depôt; but it should be stated, that to keep the buildings in repair is more costly than the amount paid for rent; in fact, so little is received by the owners, that the largest house-proprietor in A., and who is supposed to own nearly one-fourth of the city, is by no means an opulent man. One great source of expenditure is the gardens which cover all the roofs of the city. Seen from above, the whole town is a succession of terraces, over which is spread a rank and luxuriant vegetation, looking like an irregular plain, under which the multitudinous inhabitants circulate, the streets being all of them covered in and lighted only by gratings from above. Looked at from the terraces, A. appears a subterranean city, whose noises scarcely penetrate through the superincumbent bed of earth. It is easy to walk from one end of the city to the other over the streets and over the houses, there being generally wooden steps or ladders which enable the wanderer to ascend or descend from one range of terraces to another." [Bowring's Report.] The castle, situated on a lofty artificial eminence near the centre of the city, is approached by a bridge, guarded at either extremity by gates flanked with towers. It is nearly circular in figure, is surrounded by a ditch about 60 ft. wide, and has a counterscarp 20 ft. high. [Squire.] It is strongly garrisoned, and possesses a reservoir of great depth; it likewise contains a mosque of legendary renown. Of the hundred mosques which A. is reputed to possess, the finest and most venerated by the Turks is that of Zacharias. In front of each is a paved court, with a covered fountain for the use of the worshippers in the performance of their appointed ablutions. There are also fifty musjids or oratories, the most beautiful of which, named Helawie, is supposed to have been built for Christian worship by Helena, mother of Constantine, 10 or 12 Turkish colleges, 2 public libraries, 5 mehkems or courts of justice, 5 serais or governors' palaces, 15 wakfs or religious institutions, upwards of 40 great bazaars, 60 baths, 200 fountains, 100 cafes, 2 lunatic asylums, and 31 khans occupied principally by Franks or foreigners. The pasha resides in the castle of Sheikh Abu-Bekr, at the distance of about a mile from the city. The environs of A. are fertile and well-cultivated, producing oranges, citron, jujubes, pistachio-nuts, melons, and other varieties of European fruits. The climate is generally mild and salubrious. During Colonel Squire's residence at A. in the month of May, the therm. was generally at 74°, and the wind westerly, in the day blowing fresh. The atmosphere, however, is often highly oppressive, especially when the wind blows from the NNW, NE, E, or SE; and earthquakes have more than once decimated the population. The severest part of the year is from the middle of December to the latter end of January, when frost is common. A disease peculiar to A., supposed to arise from the impregnation of the water with some noxious quality, is one of the most prevalent. It is a kind of bubo, and is known by the name of the mal d'Aleppo, or Aleppo button. Severe visitations of the plague occur at periodical intervals. Wood and charcoal are used for fuel; also camels' and sheep's dung mixed with chopped brushwood. A. is supplied with good water from two springs near the village of Heilan, 8 m. N of the city, whence it is conveyed by an aqueduct, partly on a level with the ground and partly subterraneous, and refreshed by air-shafts. It is then distributed to the public fountains, baths, seraglios, and to as many of the private houses whose owners choose to pay for it, by means of leaden and earthen pipes. This aqueduct is said to be coeval with the city In 1218, it was repaired by Malek al Dhaher, the son of the great Saladin. It is annually cleansed, in the month of May, under the direction of the cadi. This process occupies eight or nine days, during which the baths are shut up, and the inhabitants are obliged to depend upon their subterraneous reservoirs, wells, and the water of the river. The great boast of A. is its gardens, which extend nearly 12 m. in length, and are parted from each other by stone walls. As they are planted more with a view to profit than pleasure, little attention is paid to elegance, and they are mere compounds of kitchen and flower-gardens blended together, without parterres or grass-plots,— presenting a strange commixture of trees, shrubs, flowers, and esculent herbs. Aleppo owes its chief importance to commerce, but this is now, and for a long time past has been on the decline. Its local position is in many respects admirable for trade. It communicates at the distance of a few hours with the Euphrates; situated midway between the Desert and the Mediterranean, it is a convenient place of centralization for the various caravans from the East; by Damascus it maintains commercial relations with Egypt and Arabia; through the defiles of the Taurus with Armenia, Kurdistan, and the Caucasian territories; and by Bassorah with the Persian gulf. It was once the emporium of Armenia and Diarbekir,—sent caravans to Persia and Bagdad,—communicated with India, by means of the Persian gulf and Bassorah,—with Mecca and Egypt, by Damascus,—and with Europe, by Scanderun and Latakia. Much of this has passed away: yet it is still a commercial city; foreign merchants are numerous, and the British, French, and Dutch, have consuls here. The principal trade is the importation of British manufactures to the amount of about 5,000 bales a-year, each bale being valued at from 3,000 to 5,000 piastres, and a few at from 6,000 to 8,000 p. The trade in colonial merchandise used to be in the hands of the French; but in 1832, English merchants began to establish themselves here, or rather to return to a city in which they were numerous two or three generations ago. In 1836, the importations from Britain were one-fourth less than those from France; but they were in 1843 quadruple the French importations. Cloth is the principal article in which England yields the palm to her competitor, commerce having in this respect experienced an extraordinary revolution; for cloth used to be the staple article of the English trade with A. for two centuries; but now nearly all the foreign cloth used comes from Marseilles. In 1837, among the imports from England were cochineal to the value of 1,050,000 piastres; indigo, 500,000 p.; pepper and pimento, 152,000 p. The total imports from England were 15,749,470 p.; while the exports to England were only 1,153,500 p. The worst feature of the Aleppo trade, and that of Syria generally, is the want of exports. The silk of Antioch is all long reel; the cotton is good only for candle-wicks; the wools of the Taurus and the Desert, although of good quality, are, from their being dirty and unwashed, badly adapted to the English market; and thus it happens that, although the pash. of A. and the surrounding districts produce in abundance the raw materials of the staple manufactures of Great Britain, English imports are paid almost exclusively in specie and bullion; while France and Italy, enabled by vicinity of position and consequent low freights to take off the coarse cotton and the unwashed wool of Syria, have lost ground in the import trade. The result of all this is, that the exchanges have been thrown into the greatest disorder; there is a perpetual drain of specie and bullion; and monies are yearly rising in spite of all the efforts of government to keep them down. The bazaars, however, exhibit a considerable supply of Oriental produce; such as galls from Diarbekir, Persian goods, rice from India, bitumen from the Dead sea. The soap manufactories of A. produce from 500 to 1,500 tons annually, according to the crop of oil; and the soapmakers of A. supply not only all Northern Syria, but also Bagdad, Mosul, Diarbekir, Orfa, Marash, and Aintab. The manufacture of stuffs for which A. was formerly celebrated thoughout the East, is still extensive. These goods consist of silk stuffs with gold and silver thread inwrought in them; silk and cotton goods flowered and striped; and striped cottons. Many of these fabrics are graceful and beautiful. The art of dyeing them is also well-understood, the colours being bright and lasting. About 4,000 looms are employed on these stuffs, and about 4,800 hands, men and children, earning from 3 to 12 piastres per day. The total yearly produce of these looms is valued at £250,000. There are about 100 dyeing and printing-shops in A., employing about 1,500 hands. In the vast and grotesque caverns outside the gates of A. there are rope-walks, in which a number of persons are employed in the manufacture of cordage and cables. The conveyance of goods to and from the coast is by mules and camels. The journey to and from Scanderun is accomplished in summer by camels in from 5 to 9 days; by mules, in from 4 to 5 days; and the rate of carriage is from 60 to 120 piastres per cantar, or 13½ to 21 p. per cwt. The journey to and from Latakia occupies from two to three days more, and the rate of carriage is from 30 to 50 per cent. higher. The pop. of A. has been variously estimated. By Tavernier it was estimated at 258,000 souls, in the city and suburbs, in 1670; by D'Arvieux, in 1683, from 285,000 to 290,000; by Volney, in 1785, at 200,000, but he remarks that, as the city is not larger than Nantes or Marseilles, and the houses only one story in height, they do not probably exceed 100,000; by Dr. Russel, who resided here thirteen years, it was estimated at 235,000 in the middle of last century, of whom 200,000 were Turks, 30,000 Christians, and 5,000 Jews; by Seitzen this number is reduced to 150,000; whilst the Rev. Mr. Connor, in 1820, reckoned the Christian population alone at 31,600, namely: Greek catholics 14,000,—Maronites 2,000, — Syrian catholics 5,000, — Nestorians 100, — Armenian catholics 8,000, — Armenian schismatics 2,000,—and Greeks, under the patriarch of Aleppo, 500. Mr. Heugh, calculating one family, or 4 persons, for every man paying the ferdeh tax in A., estimates its pop. at 17,000 families, or 68,000 souls. And taking it at 60,000, he supposes there may be of this number 40,000 Mahommedans, 16,000 Christians, 3,500 Jews, and 500 Franks.—A. occupies the site of, and derives its name from the ancient Chaleb or Chalybon. It is probably the Berræa of the Greeks, and some have identified it with the Zobah of Scripture. In 636, after having been 700 years in the possession of the Romans, it fell into the hands of the Arabs, and shared the vicissitudes of the Caliphs till it became the capital of an independent monarchy under the house of Hamadan. Subsequently to its conquest by the Seljukian prince Taouch, in 1094, its sovereigns took an active part in the wars of the Crusades; and in 1173, two years after their accession to the throne of Egypt, they lost their independence by falling under the power of Saladin. In 1260, A. was plundered by the Moguls, and again by Timur in 1402. In 1516 it was taken from the Mamelukes by Selim I., and the following year was annexed to the Ottoman dominions. On the night of the 13th of August, 1822, A. was visited by an earthquake, which destroyed a great part of the city, and in which 8,000 of the inhabitants perished. The same convulsion nearly destroyed the port of Alexandretta, and was felt as far N as Aintab. A recurrence of the same phenomenon a few months afterwards completed the work of devastation: so that the present A. has in great part arisen out of the ruins of the A. of last century.

ALEPU' (CAPE), a promontory on the SW coast of Anatolia, in N lat. 36° 30', E long. 27° 59'. It is the continental termination of Mount Phœnix in Caria, and is identified with the ancient Cynossema. The modern name signifies 'Fox cape.' The channel betwixt this cape and the island of Rhodes is about 12 m. in breadth.

ALES, or Aliso, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Busachi, 40 m. N by W of Cagliari, near the source of the Uras. Pop. 750. It is the see of a bishop, and possesses the finest cathedral church in the island; but its site is unhealthy.
ALERIA, a v. on the E coast of Corsica, 20 m. SE of Corte, near the mouth of the Tavignano.

ALESCHKI, a town of Russia, in the Crimea, on an arm of the Dnieper, nearly opposite to Kherson, in N lat. 46° 40'. It was formerly called DNIEPROVSK; and was the Elice or Olice of the Italian merchants of the Middle ages. Pop. 1,500.

ALESSANDRIA, a province and military division of the kingdom of Sardinia, in the principality of Piedmont. The military division, on a superficies of 2,039 sq. m., had a pop. in 1838 of 595,563, or of 292 to the sq. m. On the N it is bounded by the division of Novara; on the E by Lombardy and Genoa; on the S by Genoa; and on the W by Cuneo and Torino. It comprises the civil provinces of Alessandria, Acqui, Asti, Casale, Tortona, and Voghera. The civil prov. of A. had a pop. in 1821 of 99,556; in 1838 of 110,000. It is bounded by the prov. of Casale on the N; by that of Tortona on the E; by Acqui on the S; and on the W by Acqui, Asti, and Casale. Its northern districts are hilly; and throughout the entire extent of its western confines, it is intersected by ramifications of the adjacent mountainranges. In the centre, and towards the W only, it presents a level surface. It is separated from the division of Novara on the N by the river Po, and is intersected from SW to NE by the Tanaro, which, with its tributaries the Belbo, the Bormida, and the Orba—which water the south-western districts of the prov.—falls into the Po to the NE of Bassignana. The prov. generally is fertile, although in many parts destitute of natural means of irrigation, and wanting in the clothing of wood. Its principal productions are wheat, maize, wine, fruits, madder, dyer's woad, silk, and lint. It has some marble quarries, and a saline spring. It is divided into the ten arrondissements of Valenze, Bassignano, Alexandria, Bosco, Sezze, Cassine, Castellazzo, Oviglio, Felizzano, and St. Salvador. ALE

ALESSANDRIA, or ALEXANDRIA, the cap. of the above prov., and one of the strongest fortresses in Europe, is situated 65 m. by road, 46 m. direct distance, ESE of Turin; 60 m. by road, and 48 m. direct distance, SSW of Milan; and 40 m. direct N by W of Genoa; in N lat. 44° 54', E long. 8° 38'; on the banks of the Tanaro and the Bormido. It extends across the narrow marshy tract formed by the confluence of these rivers; and has an elevation of 203 ft. above sealevel. This city—which has been styled the Boulevard of Piedmont-was, until recently, enclosed on three sides by a strongly fortified wall; while extensive outworks ran along the eastern side of the Tanaro; and on the opposite or W side of that river is the citadel, which is connected with the city by means of a stone bridge. In the subjoined sketch A is the citadel; b the Ponte del Tanaro; c the Piazza Grande; d the cathedral; e the Piazza d'Arme; f the Porta Marengo; g the Canale di Scarico della Bormida. The principal buildings with which A. is adorned are the town and government houses, which are situated in a handsome square decorated with trees,the cathedral,—several parish and collegiate churches, -three hospitals, an orphan asylum, and a gymnasium. In 1806, its pop. was estimated at 35,216; at present it does not exceed 30,200, of whom 550 are Jews. The central position of this city with respect to Milan, Genoa, and Turin, its command of the Tanaro and Bormida, and of several of the most important routes of communication with the surrounding districts, render it one of considerable commercial influence and resort. It has some spinning mills, and manufactories of silk, linen, cotton, and wax-candles. Its fairs, held in the end of April and beginning of October, are among the most important in Italy. A. will form the central point of the three great trunk or principal railway lines of Sardinia, one of which is to pass, by way of Genoa, across the Apennines; the second, by way of Turin, to Asli; and the third, by Valenza and Novarra, to the Lago Maggiore. It is the seat of a court of justice, under the jurisdiction of the senate of Turin; and is the residence of a bishop who is a suffragan of the arch. of Verceil. The name by which it is now distinguished was substituted for that of Cesarea, by the Lombard league—who built it as a barrier against the Ghibelline party—in honour of Pope Alexander III., who conferred upon the city the ecclesiastical dignity which it still possesses. The appellation Della Paglia, [i. e., 'of Straw,'] was probably conferred upon it in derision by the imperialists. A. was taken by Sforza, Duke of Milan, in 1522; sustained an unsuccessful siege by the French in 1657; and after an obstinate resistance fell into their hands in 1707. In 1796 it made a conditional surrender to Bonaparte. In 1799 it fell before the combined armies of Austria and Russia; but after the battle of Marengo in 1800 was regained by the French, who expended immense sums upon its fortifications, and retained it until 1814, when the prov. became a portion of the Sardinian dominions, and the fortifications were to a great extent razed.
ALESSANDRIA, a town of Sicily, in the prov. of Girgenti, 19 m. N by W of Girgenti, near the source of the Maccassoli.
ALESSANDRO, or North Isle, the most northerly of the Sulphur group of islets, to the NE of the Philippines, in N lat. 25° 15', E long. 141° 5'.
ALESSANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. and 18 m. SSW of Otranto. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Otranto.

ALESSIO, a kadilik and town of Albania. The kadilik forms the S part of the pashalik of Skutari, and includes the mountain-districts inhabited by the Miriditi and Madiani. The town is built on the l. bank of the Drin, near its mouth. It is called LESH by the Turks. The famous Scanderbeg is buried in the church, now a mosque, which is included within the walls of the fortress. A. is the ancient Lissus of Pliny, or Elissus.
ALESSIO (Punte Santo), an abrupt bold cliff on the E coast of Sicily, 8 m. SW 3 W of Cape Grosso.
ALESSO, a lake in Lombardy, 18 m. NNW of Udine, between Mount Palis and Mount Simeon. When swollen by rain it discharges its superfluous waters into the Tagliamento and the Palarda.

ALET, or Aleth, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Aude, on the Aude river, 6 m. SE of Limoux. Pop. 1,000. Its baths are in repute. They are supplied by four springs, three of which are cold, and one warm. Copper and iron occur in the vicinity.

ALETSH, a glacier of Switzerland, in the Haut Valais, descending from the Jungfrau on its S. side. It would appear that the Aletsh has made considerable advances on the lower grounds, or has increased greatly, as the inhabitants of the district, two or three centuries ago, used to take their children across the lower site of this glacier, to Grindelwald for baptism.

ALEUTIAN, or ALEUTAN ARCHIPELAGO, called also the ALEOUTES, and the ALEUTIC, or ALEUTSKY ISLANDS—names all severally derived from the Russian aleut, signifying 'a bold rock,'a chain of islands in the N. Pacific, which extends, in a curved line, from the peninsula of Kamtschatka in Asia on the W, to Cape Alashka on the SW of Russian America, and which separates Behring's sea from the N. Pacific ocean. The chain stretches from 163 deg. W long. to 166 deg. E long.,—a distance on their parallel of above 600 m.; and extends from the 53d parallel in the centre to the 55th at either extremity; their medium lat, being 52 deg. 30 min. These isles are of very various dimensions, graduating from 104 m.,—the computed length of Unalashka, which is the largest—to mere rocky islets. Their number is still uncertain; but of the 40 which have been named, the principal are Behring's or Commodore island, which approaches nearest to the coast of Asia, Copper island, and Attu and Agattu, all in the most westerly or Aleutian group, originally so called in distinction from the central and eastern groups. Buldhuir, Tagalak, Tagana, Kuska, Amchitka, Kanaga, Adagh or Adak, Atchu, and Amlak or Amlia, are the most important of the islands, forming the central or Andrean group. Amoughta or Amukta, Atshak, Unalashka, Akutan, Akun, Unimak, and Sannak are the principal of the Fox islands, the most easterly of the chain. These insular groups are formed apparently by the summits of a great range of submarine mountains of a highly volcanic character, some presenting extinct craters, while others are active volcanoes, and all are liable to frequent earthquakes. In 1795 a volcanic island rose from the sea in the middle of the line, which, in 1807, was found to be enlarged to about 20 m. in circumference, and lava was then flowing down its sides. They all present a barren and desolate aspect: black, conical mountains, rising perpendicularly from the sea, and rearing their snow-capped summits to the clouds, form their most prominent features. Their coasts are low, and surrounded with hidden reefs, which, with the rapid tides which set in amongst them, and the prevalence of fogs, render the navigation of some of the numerous channels with which the groups are intersected extremely dangerous. Captain Beechey entering the Pacific from the Kamtschatkan sea passed between the SW angle of Unimak and the island of Cugalga, had no soundings with 40 fath., until about 4 m. off Unimak, where he found 30 fath, on a bank of dark-coloured lava, pebbles, and scorize, but immediately lost it again, and had no bottom afterwards. [Voyage to the Pacific, p. 341.] He remarks that these islands in the autumn appear to be enveloped in fog about half-way down, and to have a region of mist lying to windward. The winter, considering the high latitude of the archipelago, is mild; but the ice rarely disappears before the month of ALE May; and the summer is short, although during the brief period of its duration it is warm and serene. Captain Beechey found these islands covered about two-thirds of the way down with snow on the 14th of October, 1827. The thermometer rarely rises in Unalashka above + 15 deg. R. in summer; while in winter it seldom falls below — 15 deg. R. Lutke estimates the mean temperature of Unalashka at + 3.5 R. The mountains contain porphyry of a fine grain, and iron; and traces of copper have been discovered in the island, which bears the name of that mineral. The A. islands generally are well-watered by numerous lakes and streams; and the soil in the more sheltered valleys is fertile; but with the exception of grass, different species of the Rubus and Vaccinium, and a few pot-herbs, there is little cultivation. The climate, however, promotes a most luxuriant herbage. Chamisso says, the grass on Unalashka was so strong that it was difficult to walk through it; and, except in Brazil, Lutke says he knew no country where the growth of grass was so abundant. Excepting in the Fox group, in which some stunted firs, birches, aspens, oaks, and willows, are to be found, these islands are destitute of trees or plants surpassing the dimensions of low shrubs and bushes; but Langsdorff says that large timber is often floated hither from America, the South sea islands, and Japan.—The quadrupeds common to all the groups are bears, reindeer, wolves, ermine, beavers, and a few others. Foxes of various species were formerly very numerous, and their skins formed an important article of trade, but they are now nearly exterminated. The feathered tribes, especially wild geese and ducks, are numerous; and the sea abounds with fish. The seal is particularly valuable to the inhabitants of these desolate isles. It affords them a constant supply of food and clothing; and the thin membrane of the entrails is converted by them into a substitute for glass. Whales and porpoises frequent the coast, but are not numerous; salmon, herring, and halibut, are very abundant.—The Aleutes are of middle size, stout, but well-proportioned: the men are of a dark brown, healthy complexion, with small black eyes, forming a sort of link between the Mongol Tartars and North Americans. [Langsdorf.] Their features, although not symmetrical, are strongly marked, and have an agreeable and benevolent expression. Their hair, which they cut short and keep shorn on the top of the head, is black and wiry, and they have little beard. Sauer estimated their number at 1,100 males, including children, or about 2,300 toward the close of last cent. Veniaminov more recently estimated the whole pop. at 1,500. The women are generally of lighter complexion, with reddish fair hair. Tattooing is now less common; but they still continue the practice of inserting rings into their nostrils and under lips. They wear the hair over the forehead, and gather it into a tuft at the back. The dress of both sexes consists of seals' skins, formed into a sort of carter's frock or shirt, fastened round the neck with a broad stiff collar. That of the women is decked with glass beads, or the beaks of seaparrots; and of the men, surmounted with a collar of feathers or goat's hair. The women wear bracelets of glass beads above the wrist and ankle joints. The practice of boring the under lip a little below the mouth, and sticking various objects through the slits so made, was once common, but is now nearly extinguished. Though reputed savages, the Aleutians are far from being deficient in mental capacity; and their various handicrafts sufficiently testify their ingenuity. They are even mild and polite in their demeanour, and extremely hospitable. The missionary Veniaminov, who resided for above ten years on Unalashka, declares that he never witnessed any fight or affray of a serious character amongst the natives. Their religious ideas are extremely vague: they seem to consist of a species of demonolatry. The Christianity of the Greek church is however said to be making considerable progress amongst them. Veniaminov drew up a grammar of the Aleutian language, which was published at St, Petersburg in 1834. Polygamy is common with both sexes; wives are obtained by purchase, and may be repudiated or exchanged at pleasure. The principal occupations of the men consist in fishing and hunting, which constitute their chief means of subsistence, and in the construction of their dwellings, which in summer are huts, and in winter spacious excavations of the earth, roofed with turf, and capable of affording accommodation to from 50 to 150 individuals. They are skilful fishers and hunters, and in pursuit of the sea-otter will undertake voyages of 1,000 m. in length, from island to island of the archipelago, or along the American coast, in their fragile baidares, a kind of light boat composed of the skins of animals drawn tightly over a frame-work of wood or of fish-bones. In form these vessels are long and narrow, being about 18 ft. in length by 2 in breadth, and commonly made to hold only one person, who sits in a round hole just fitted to the size of the body. [Langsdorf.] Cook says their canoes are the smallest in use on the whole west coast of America; but he describes them as only 12 ft. long, and 12 broad in the middle. The people of Kadiak and Unalashka also engage with great boldness in whale-fishing. All domestic employments, and the making of clothes, the covering of canoes, and forming various useful articles of wicker work, are assigned to the women. The natives are obliged to give their services to the Russian American company for a period of three years; but the Creoles, or children of Russian fathers by native women, enjoy all the usual privileges of Russian citizens. The inhabitants of this archipelago, since they became the victims of the aggression of the Russian traders about the middle of last century, have been rapidly decreasing in number, and many of the islands are now entirely depopulated.—The discovery of this chain of islands is attributed to Behring and Tchirikoff in 1741. In 1768-9 they were visited by Krenitzine and Levachef; but it was not until the close of the century, when they were visited by Billings and Saritchef, that any complete exploration of them was effected. The whole group is included in the Russian government of Irkutsk; but was made over to the Russian American company in 1826, who divided them into two districts, -one called the Atkha district, including all the islands to the W of Unaska, and ander the direction of a factory established on the island of Atkha,—and the other called the Unalashka district, extending over the remainder of the range, the peninsula of Alashka as far as the meridian of 160 deg. W, and the islands of St. Paul and St. George in the sea of Behring, 190 m. from Unalashka.—Coxe's Russian Discoveries, 8vo., 1803.—Langsdorff's Voyages during 1803-7, 4to.—Beechey's Voyage to the Pacific and Behring's Straits, 4to., 1831.—Wrangell's Letters.

DERIEH, formerly the cap. and still one of the principal cities of Egypt, is situated in N lat. 31° 12', E long. 29° 54′, 14 m. W of the Canopic or most western branch of the Nile, and 100 m. NW of Cairo, direct. The shortest route to Cairo is about 110 m.; while the distance by water, by the Canal of Mahmoodiëh to Atfeh, and thence by the Nile to Cairo, is 172 m. A. occupies a narrow neck of land, extending into the sea to a peninsula or long bank of sand which runs nearly parallel with the shore, at the distance of half-a-mile, and forms the two ports, having the Mediterranean to the N, and Lake Mareotis to the S. From its situation without the Delta, and the nature of its dry arid soil, A. may rather be regarded as a town of the Libyan desert than as belonging to Egypt. The harbour is bad and difficult of access, owing to rocks at the entrance, and, as some imagine, to the ruins of the celebrated lighthouse called the Pharos, built upon an island of the same name, lying about 7 furlongs from the mainland, but united to it by a mole called anciently the Heptastadium, which also forms the line of separation between the old and new harbours. Until the present pasha's administration, no Christian vessel was allowed to enter the old or western harbour, which, being the safest and most accessible, was appropriated to Turkish vessels: at present both harbours are open to vessels of every country. Close to the Pharos, the depth of water is about 6 fathoms; and from 51 to 4 along the whole shore to the point of Eunostus at the entrance of the western harbour; but the shoals and reefs render it necessary to employ a pilot, in order to effect an entrance, which is not to be attempted in the night, even with the assistance of such a guide. The main or central channel has 5 or 6 fathoms; the Marabut, from 4½ to 6; others 4 to 6, but very narrow,—the widest being not quite 1,500 ft. The deepest part of the harbour, about due W and due N of the catacombs, is 10 to 11 fath.; close in, to within 200 ft. of the shore, 4 to 6 fath.; and under the town itself, at little more than a cable's length from the shore, 3 to 4 fath. There is an old lighthouse on the site of the Pharos; and another was more recently erected by Mahommed Ali, on the point of Eunostus. The dockyard occupies a site of about 60 acres, and possesses a fine frontage to the sea-coast of about \( \frac{1}{4} \) of a mile in length. It has slips for line-of-battle ships and frigates. The changes which the coast has undergone have probably materially affected the safety of this harbour: yet still, says Sir Robert Wilson, "A. must be pronounced the key to Egypt, since in its harbour alone security can be found for shipping of any burden throughout the year." The general appearance of A. is by no means striking, unless perhaps from the sea. From the landside it appears like a stone-mason's yard, with nothing to relieve the eye from sun, sand, and stone, except a few palm-trees, and the occasional flag of a consulate. "The impression produced by the first view of the interior of the city of A.," says Lord Nugent, "is one of melancholy, which deepens into deadly weariness on further acquaintance with its details. The filth of its streets and suburbs, -the squalid, unhealthy, penury-stricken look of its population,—the unfinished condition of the new buildings, and the ruinous condition of the old, -everything has an air of neglect, of suffering under discouragement which has quenched all energy, all power, and desire to struggle against it." . . "It has neither the cheerful welcome of a populous city, nor the romantic solitude of a ruin or a wilderness." ['Lands Classical and Sacred.'] "The ruins of A.," says Mr. St. John, "are indeed ruins. Babylon itself, which it once nearly rivalled in grandeur, can scarcely be said to have left fewer vestiges of its existence and magnificence; for, beyond the precincts of the modern town, you behold, far and near, upon the plain, nothing but irregular mounds of rubbish or sand, which may probably conceal the substructions or foundations of ancient edifices. Here and there, when the pasha's workmen have been digging among these mounds for stones, you in fact discover the foundations of various Greek or Roman buildings, in stone or brick, with arched passages, portions of old cisterns, fragments of pillars, or perhaps entire columns of a pale red granite, overthrown or half-buried in the sand. Were the whole of these prodigious heaps of rubbish cleared away, private houses, little less entire than those of Pompeii, might perhaps be discovered. . . In the midst of these prostrate remains of the ancient city, we find, thinly scattered, the modern dwellings of the actual lords of the soil, of which some are large houses, in the Turkish style of architecture, others the meanest cabins in which poverty and wretchedness ever took shelter. The constant intercourse kept up with Europeans-who at present constitute a large proportion of the population—the almost general abandonment of the turban, the absence of articles of national manufacture in the bazaars, greatly diminish the romantic interest which, under other circumstances, an Eastern city seems calculated to inspire. Swanston Ed duced one exceedingly beneficial effect on the manners of the Egyptians; no description of raiment, sity; the half-naked negro from Darfur, the muslinclad Hindoo, the pompous Persian, the gorgeous Greek, and the plain Englishman,-all passing unheeded through the streets of Alexandria and Cairo, where the most clownish Fellah, the most impertinent sloven, the silliest barber, is never betrayed into an offensive laugh or stare at the stranger." The appearance of the surrounding country undergoes considerable variation according to the season. In autumn, the whole surface of the soil in the environs exhibits a naked sterile appearance; but in spring, especially after copious winter-rains, viewed from any commanding height, the whole country exhibits "a luxuriant and smiling appearance, every green hollow and swelling undulation being sprinkled with wild flowers, impregnating the air with their short-lived fragrance." [St. John.] The environs present a number of tasteful country-seats with well-enclosed gardens. In the Turkish quarter of the city, the streets are narrow and irregular, with an occasional mixture of the Oriental latticed windows and Saracenic arches. The houses are built of brick or of stone. They exhibit a gloomy appearance, from the small number of windows to the street, and often present only a closely reticulated wooden grating instead of glass. The habitations of the poor, constituting a large proportion of the whole, are rudely built of stone and bricks dug up from the ruins of the old city, and seldom exceed 6 or 8 ft. in height, while in length and width they are not above 8 or 10 ft. Olin.] In the quarter of the Franks or Europeans, situated at the extremity of the town furthest from the new port—owing to the restriction already men- . tioned—the houses are clean and whitewashed, and generally elegant and well-built in broad airy streets, A number of these edifices are immense quadrangles. three or four stories high, with long galleries running around the interior court, to which the inmates ascend by flights of stairs rising from the enclosed area. The arsenals and palaces upon the Ras-el-Tyn, as well as several extensive hospitals and manufactories, are structures of this description. [Olin.] The city has lately been increased by the construction of a large square, in which are found the principal hotels and most of the consulates; and it is surrounded by turreted walls between 30 and 40 ft. high, with four gates, and extensive outworks. Only a small part of the area enclosed by the walls is occupied with buildings: the rest is covered with ruins, interspersed with a few verdant spots cultivated as gardens. It was to the circumstance of Alexandria forming a link in the chain of intercourse between Europe and the East that this city owed its eminence originally: to the discovery of the passage to India by the Cape of Good Hope it owed its decline; and to the recent restoration of the original route it owes its present importance and very rapid growth. There is regular intercourse by steam between Alexandria and Southampton, as well as with Malta, Gibraltar, Marseilles, Trieste, and the whole of the Levant. The mail from Falmouth has been landed at A. within 14 days. The steamer from Malta generally makes the passage—820 m.—in three days. Alexandria to Malta, The transit between Alexandria and Cairo is effected by means of a steam-tug on the Mahmoudiëh canal to Atfeh, in 8 hours, and thence per steam up the Nile to Cairo, in 15 hours; thence by wheeled carriages to Suez in about 16 hours; at the expense of £13 first class for adults, £8 for children, £7 second class,— the whole expense from England to Calcutta, for a berth in the general cabin, being £143. [Wilkinson, 1843.] This extensive intercourse has occasioned a great influx of foreigners and increase of trade, and has tended to obliterate national peculiarities. The pasha, it is understood, has purchased the steamboats and all the materiel belonging to the Peninsular and Oriental Steam Company in Egypt, and the transit through Egypt is now a monopoly in his hands. Two plans have been proposed for improving the transit to India through Egypt. One is the formation of a ship-canal from Suez across the isthmus to the Mediterranean at Tineh; the other is the construction of a railway from Suez to Cairo. These projects will be better discussed in the article Suzz. If a railroad should ever be formed through Egypt with a view to this transit trade, it seems to us probable that A. will be found the most fitting place for its commencement; but the scheme is yet far from being matured; and the first great improvement on the existing means of transit will probably be effected on the side of Europe.
