It may be questioned, however, whether an overland route by the gulf of Genoa will not be found preferable to that by the Adriatic. The editor of one of the most intelligent of our metropolitan journals says on this subject: "By measurements, which we have obtained from official sources, the distances of the two. voyages are as follow:- From Alexandria to Trieste, by Corfu, ...... 1,220 g. m. to Genoa, by Messina, ..... 1,313 by Malta,..... 1,404 - Assuming the average velocity of the steamers employed to be 10 m. an hour, the voyage to Trieste would be performed in 122 hours; that to Genoa, by Messina, in 131, or by Malta in 140 hours. Assuming for the present that the estimates of the time required for the land-journey on either route which we gave in our former article were correct, there are 17 hours in favour of the land-route from Genoa to London over that from Trieste to London. If, therefore, Malta is left unvisited, there remains a difference of 7 hours in favour of the route by Genoa, even in the actual circumstances of the roads between London and Trieste on the one hand and Genoa on the other. There is further to be taken into consideration, that one great objection always urged to the substitution of the Trieste for the present overland route has been the necessity it involved of diminishing the importance of Malta as the centre of British power in the Mediterranean. From the measurements now quoted, it appears that the route by Genoa would admit of Malta remaining the great centre of British communications, and add only one hour to the time required for accomplishing the journey by Trieste—an addition quite unappreciable in so long a journey. . . Au reste, there is ample room for the development of steam-boat and railway travelling and carriage on both routes; and England has an interest in the prosperity of both. The improvement of the Trieste route will facilitate our intercourse with Corfu, and extend our commercial relations with the highly capable regions subject to the Austrian sceptre. The improvement of the Genoa route will facilitate our intercourse with Malta, and (we believe) with British India, and enable England by legitimate means to become the rock upon which the liberties of Switzerland and Italy will stand secure." Of the trade of Egypt a great proportion is concentrated in A.; and the pursuits of the inhabitants are chiefly mercantile. There are 10 considerable English houses; but the French, Italian, and Greeks produce, and executing his commissions in various countries. The most important articles of commerce are cotton, gum, rice, &c., and the other productions of the East, in return principally for articles of European manufacture. In 1831 the greatest amount of exports was to Turkey, and next to Austria; the imports also were largest from these countries. In 1838, Consul Thorburn reported that the export trade of A. with Europe was conducted almost exclusively on European bottoms; and that the mercantile shipping belonging to the port consisted of 16 vessels of from 100 to 300 tons. In 1840, the value of imports was £1,327,396, whereof £439,576 was from England, Malta, and the Ionian isles; and the value of exports, £1,072,033, whereof £172,139 was to these countries. According to the annual Alexandria Custom-house report, it appears that during the year 1846 the total value of exports was £1,900,000 sterling; and of imports, £1,750,000 sterling. Both of imports and exports England had by far the greatest share. There were exported from Egypt to England, besides other minor articles: The imports from Great Britain included the following articles: Cotton manufactures, £323,460. Coals, 40,000 tons, besides the quantity imported for the use of the East India company, and the Peninsular and Oriental company. Cochineal, £11,520; indigo, £20,000; hardware, £13,800; earthenware, £6,670; tin-plates, £4,360; sugar, £6,000; wines and spirits, £9,000 sterling. The total exports to the United Kingdom amounted to £651,000 And the imports to . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 615,000 During 1846, 1,218 vessels of all nations entered the port of Alexandria, and 1,225 left it. Of these 251 were English entering, and 220 leaving. This great amount of traffic, however, affords no correct index to the actual state of the inhabitants of Alexandria, in as much as the pasha monopolizes nearly the whole trade of Egypt, and all the adjacent countries to which his influence extends,—a practice which has ruined many who were formerly opulent, and has reduced almost to beggary the smaller traders and the Fellahs or peasants. Even the few Frank merchants who have prospered, it is said, have done so not in the ordinary course of commerce, but rather as agents of the pasha, and by some fortunate speculations in cotton which they have taken in payment of their advances. The whole produce of Egypt and of the countries under the influence of the pasha, except what is necessary for the army and fleet on the Red sea, is collected at Alexandria, in the shuna or range of warehouses erected for the purpose, at prices fixed by himself, and is there publicly sold by auction, the upset price being regulated by the latest report of the markets, the merchants having the privilege of examining the articles in the shuna before the sale, and being required to pay in cash the price at which it is knocked down to him within a limited number of days. The principal articles thus disposed of are cotton, gums, coffee, indigo, linseed, which go to the markets of Trieste, Leghorn, Marseilles, and London; rice and opium to Smyrna, the Greek isles, and Constantinople. The export of corn and other articles of food has greatly diminished, the amount raised being little more than is required for home consumption. A recent treaty, indeed, ostensibly annuls this monopoly; but it has not been found difficult for despotic authority to evade the force of any restriction upon its interests. From England are imported iron, lead, coals, ordnance, cables, anchors, machinery, and some manufactured goods; from France, Switzerland, Italy, and Germany, wines, spirits, oils, manufactures of silk and cotton, articles of dress, furniture, hardware, trinkets, &c.; from Austria, timber; from Turkey and the isles, silks, tobacco, oils, and some wood and fruits. The commerce with Malta and the Barbary states is chiefly in grain, and is inconsiderable. -The weights in common use are the rotal or rut'l, estimated at 153 oz. avoird., and equal to 144 dirh'ems or drachmæ; but a rotal of spun cotton is equal to 180 drachmæ; and a rotal of Syrian silk contains 229 drachmæ. The oke is equal to about 23 lbs. avoird., and contains 400 drachma. The Ckunta'r or cantar is equal to 100 rotals.—The dirhem or drachm is equal to from 47.5 to 48 English grains. The ardeb varies from 5 to 8 English bushels. [Yates.] of Constantinople, of 20 piastres 5 paras. Roman empire, it was reckoned at 300,000 free persons, the slaves being probably equal in number. It sunk to 6,000 after the discovery of the passage to India by the Cape of Good Hope; but within the last few years the increase is so rapid as to render probable a return to the highest number hitherto attained. Thus about the year 1825 it was reckoned at 16,000; in 1837 at 40,000, of whom 3,000 were English, Maltese, and Ionians; in 1840 at 60,000, not including the garrison, 8,000 men, or the sailors of the fleet, 12,000. Browne says the Alexandrians are remarkable for the facility with which they acquire different languages; but their own Arabic is impure, being mingled with Turkish and other dialects. There is but little intercourse between the natives and the Franks except upon business. Latterly, some of the wealthier Frank merchants have obtained grants of land and built houses and gardens; but the principal architect is Ibrahim Pacha, whose building speculations have been very extensive. The climate of A. is agreeable, the heat of summer being seldom above 85°, and tempered by the Etesian or NW winds which prevail during 9 months in the year. Yet, owing to other causes, its climate is pronounced by some physicians the most unwholesome in all Egypt. The principal of these appears to be the vicinity of Lake Mareotis, now a saline swamp. [Madden.] A considerable quantity of rain falls in the winter; and A. is at all times very damp, the atmosphere being saturated with a saline vapour which condenses on the walls and furniture of the houses in small crystals; yet diseases of the lungs are unknown. But the city is seldom perfectly free from the plague, which may be in part owing to the want of proper ventilation and cleanliness in the streets and dwellings, as well as to the personal habits of the people, whose frequent ablutions, though enjoined as a part of their religion, are performed in so perfunctory a manner as to comply merely with the letter of the command,—hence ensues a great liability to disease when exposed to any malady of a communicable character, or to unhealthy atmospherical influences. Fevers are said to be rare: diarrhœa, dysentery, and ophthalmia, more frequent. The average yearly mortality is said to be 7.3 per cent. [Bowring.] European residents generally enjoy as good health as in their own country; but the offspring of such as settle in the country are cadaverous and unsightly. [St. John.] Alexandria contains naval and military hospitals, under the direction of European physicians; also a quarantine board under the superintendence of the consular body, with a large lazaretto outside the walls. Vessels arriving from any infected port of the Levant are subjected to the same quarantine as in Europe. The municipal government is intrusted to a governor, who has under him an officer called the bashagu, or chief police magistrate, whose duty is to preserve order and quiet. The city is divided into quarters under the presidency of sheikhs responsible to the governor: each trade and profession has its sheikh, whose duty is to collect the taxes and see to the conduct of the members. Guard-houses are distributed over the city, to which the military may take all disorderly persons on whom the officer on native; but if a Frank he must be sent to the consul of his nation to be dealt with according to the law of his country. These regulations cause the city to be very orderly, though distinguished, in the earlier part of its history, and even down to modern times, for the turbulent and seditious character of the inhabitants. Besides the police court, there is the mehkemeh or kadi's court, where all civil questions between natives are determined; and a commercial court, with Frank judges, and a Turkish president for the trial of questions between Franks and natives. Among the objects most worthy of attention in Alexandria, are the obelisks called Cleopatra's needles, marking the site of the Cæsareum or Cæsar's temple, one of which is standing, and is a fine piece of granite about 70 ft. high, and 7\frac{1}{2} ft. square at the base. The other is fallen and half-buried in the sand; its length is about 66 ft. Pompey's pillar-why so called is uncertain, since it is now generally admitted by the learned to have had no connection with the Roman general whose name it bears—is 99 ft. 4 in. high, including capital, base, and pedestal, the shaft being 67 ft. 7 in.; and is supposed from its form at the top to have supported a statue, or perhaps a cinerary urn. It is about 2 m. from the shore. The catacombs, formed by excavations in the calcareous rock, of which the shore is composed, are remarkable for their extent, and for the elegance of one of the chambers. Among other remarkable objects are a tower, called the Roman tower, the remains of the Saracenic walls, and some large vaulted cisterns underneath the houses, coated with a thick red plaster impermeable to water, and originally designed to be and still used as reservoirs of water received from the Nile by means of the Canopic canal which, in early times, supplied A. with water, but into which the water, no longer flowing through the old aqueducts, is brought at the season of inundation on the backs of camels. Among the principal buildings are several mosques,-one called the mosque of 1,001 columns,—another the mosque of St. Athanasius, from which was taken a sarcophagus, called the tomb of Alexander, now in the British museum,—the convent of St. Mark, belonging to the Copts, and another belonging to the Latin church,—the pasha's palace, which may be seen by an order,—the arsenal and ships of war,—and the bazaars. History.] During the visit of Alexander the Great to Egypt, in the year B.C. 332, after the conquest of Syria, he was struck with the situation of a small town called Ruacotis, and, with intuitive sagacity, fixed on it as the position of a city designed to be the link of communication between Europe, Asia, and Africa. Without delay, therefore, he proceeded to define the limits in the form, it is said, of a Macedonian mantle; and so eagerly was he bent on the undertaking that, when the chalk used for the purpose was exhausted, he completed the outline with the flour designed for the workmen's provision, which a multitude of birds having immediately devoured, the omen was interpreted to foreshow the future resort of men from all nations to the city he was about to construct. This legend, whether fabulous or otherwise, indicates the promptness and determination with which the work was undertaken, and the sanguine anticipations of success that should attend it. The foundation having been laid under the direction of Dinochares or Dinocrates, Alexander gave his own name to the city, whose subsequent history realized or surpassed all that could have entered even the sanguine mind of the Conqueror. The original extent of the city is said to have been about 30 stadia or furlongs long by 7 or 8 wide; and it is said to have contained two of the noblest streets in the world, intersecting each other at right angles, some traces of which are still discoverable. On the death of Alexander, Egypt having fallen to the share of Ptolemy, Alexandria rose constantly and rapidly in importance. It became at once the emporium for trade, and the nursery of learning. Here Ptolemy Soter laid the foundation of that vast library which by successive additions, we are told, attained the amount of 700,000 volumes in two collections, one in the museum accidentally destroyed by fire during the occupation of the city by Julius Cæsar, the other in the Serapion, or temple of Serapis, (second in magnificence only to the Capitol of Rome,) reserved for destruction in 640 in obedience to the well known dictum of the Caliph Omar, that "if the books agree with the book of God, they are useless and need not be preserved; if they disagree, they are pernicious and ought to be destroyed:" a story, however, which rests on no very valid foundation, which is controverted by the Moslem historians, and is certainly in no accordance with the conduct of the Arabs soon after this period, who became the principal depositories of learning, and preserved it from retrogression if they did not promote its advancement when elsewhere it was almost extinct. It is not doubtful, however, that the library was vast, and that it was destroyed. In this city was completed by order of Ptolemy Philadelphus, in the year B. C. 277, the Greek translation of the Jewish Scriptures called the Septuagint. The palace of ancient Alexandria contained the museum, which seems to have corresponded very much to our idea of a university, not so much for the education of youth, as for the cultivation of literature by learned residents, perhaps bearing some analogy to the fellowships of modern times. From this institution emanated many men of distinguished learning and talent; and different ages of the world, and different departments of human thought, have been indebted to Alexandria for their brightest ornaments. Here, it is recorded, was the earliest Christian school, ascribed to the evangelist St. Mark; and here, in later ages, was the chief seat of that peculiar modification of Christianity and Platonism, called New Platonism, or the Alexandrian school, which could number among its members the illustrious but apparently incongruous names of Porphyry and Plotinus, Origen and Ammonius. Its modern history, as identified with that of Egypt, will be related in another place. It may suffice to remark here, that exposed to violent shocks and commotions at the interval of ages, this city has nevertheless owed its actual state of advancement or decline to the peaceful energies of commerce, waxing or waning, according as it has been or has ceased to be in the pathway of the merchant. ALEXANDRIA (CANAL OF), or the MAHMOUDIEH CANAL, & work executed by the present pasha of Egypt with the view of establishing a water-communication between the city of Alexandria and the Rosetta branch of the Nile at Fouah, so as to avoid the dangerous passage over the bogaz or bar of Rosetta, and expedite the transit to and from Cairo. It is 48 m. in length, extending in a serpentine course from the harbour of A. to Atfieh near Fouah; and was when first completed 90 ft. in breadth, and from 15 ft. to 18 ft. deep. Its mean depth is considerably above the level of the Nile. During high Nile what it was hoped would prove a sufficiency of water is admitted, and prevented from escaping by sluices at either end. No bridges traverse it in its whole course. The work was accomplished within the space of 12 months, in 1819-20, by the labour of 250,000 men, of whom 23,000 perished under the severity of their task and from want of food and pure water. It consisted mainly of cleaning out the bed of an ancient canal which existed on this line, and had been used so late as the time of the Venetians, and in shutting out the reflux of the sea into Lake Mareotis. Its appearance is uninteresting; the banks being unrelieved by any diversity of scenery, and the view on both sides extending over a dreary plain whose level is only interrupted here and there by mounds of rubbish, the debris of ancient cities. In some places, where the inequalities of the surface have been artificially filled up, the canal runs over a broad causeway elevated above the surrounding plain. Twentyfour towers erected as guard-houses and signal-stations are situated at equal distances on its banks. It is also understood to be already rendered nearly useless by the rapid accumulation of mud which has been allowed to form in its channel, chiefly in consequence of the insufficient slope of its banks. It was likewise designed to supply Alexandria with water, and to irrigate the lands on both sides. But not having been at the outset excavated to a sufficient depth, direct communication with the Nile is necessarily cut off when the river is low, that is in spring; and at the same season the water towards Alexandria becomes brackish from the infiltration of that of Lake Marcotis, which is separated from the canal by a very imperfect embankment. While in use passengers on the Indian route were conveyed by this canal in an iron boat of 130 tons, tracked by four horses, to Atfieh on the Nile, whence they proceeded by an iron steamer to Bulak the port of Cairo, a distance varying according to the season and the height of the river from 120 to 130 m., but usually accomplished against the stream running from 2 to 44 m. in 20 hours. The project of forming a railway nearly in the line of this canal has been latterly entertained by the pasha.
ALEXANDRIA, a v. in the p. of Bonhill, Dumbartonshire, on the W bank of the Leven, 31 m. N of Dumbarton. The pop. of the quoad sacra parish of A. was returned at 3,397 in 1841. Inhabited houses 641.

ALEXANDRIA, a co. of the U.S., which lately comprised all that part of the district of Columbia which lay W of the Potomac, which formerly belonged to Virginia, and was in 1846 retroceded to that state. Its area is about 36 sq. m. A wooden bridge of above 1 m. in length crossing the Potomac connects it with the city of Washington. The laws of Virginia are in force here, unless superseded by the special enactments of the general government. Pop. in 1830, 9,608; in 1840, 9,967, of whom 1,374 were slaves, and 1,862 free coloured.—The cap. of the co., bearing the same name, is finely situated on the W side or r. bank of the Potomac, 7 m. below Washington, 43 m. SSW of Baltimore; in N lat. 38° 49', W long. 77° 4'. [American Alm., 1848.] It is considerably elevated, ascending gradually from the river which has here a depth of water sufficient for vessels of the largest class. The tonnage of the port in 1840 was 14,470. The exports are wheat, Indian corn, and tobacco. The Chesapeake and Ohio canal extends to this place; and steam boats ply hourly to and from Washington. The museum contains more personal relics of General Washington than can be found elsewhere. - Also a town in Grafton co., New Hampshire, U. S., 34 m. NW of Concord. ALEXANDRIA. See ALESSANDRIA and ALEKSANDRIA.

ALEXANDRINA (LAKE), a shallow sheet of water or lagune in South Australia, of from 50 to 60 m. in length, and from 30 to 40 m. in breadth, lying immediately to the eastward of the gulf of St. Vincent, and extending southwards to near the shore of of Amboyna, but not published, which describes them as "a large, ALF Encounter bay, with which it communicates, but only by a passage impracticable even to small boats. Its medium depth is only about 4 ft., and the tidal rise does not exceed 8 inches. There is a large bight on it to the SE; and an extensive bay on the NW. The Murray river discharges itself into this lake. The country for 40 m. around, on the N and E, is low, and appears to have once formed a part of the lake.
ALEXISBAD, a watering-place in the valley of the Selke in the Hartz district of Anhalt-Bernburg. The water is a strong chalybeate.
ALEYOR, a town of Minorca, 6 m. NW of Mahon. Pop. 3,000.
ALF, a v. at the mouth of the vale of the Issbuch, on the Moselle, in Rhenish Prussia, 6 m. from Bertrich baths.
